summary:
Coptic Christianity (founded c. 1st century AD, traditionally by the Evangelist Mark in Alexandria) is the indigenous Christian tradition of Egypt — one of the oldest continuous Christian communities in the world, which maintained its distinct theological identity through the great controversies of early Christian history and has preserved an unbroken thread of Egyptian Christianity from antiquity to the present day. Alexandria was one of the greatest centers of early Christian intellectual life, producing theologians like Origen, Clement of Alexandria, and Athanasius who shaped the development of Christian doctrine globally.

The defining moment in Coptic history was the [[event:2105|Council of Chalcedon]] (451 AD), where the Egyptian church rejected the council's two-natures Christology (that Christ has two distinct natures, divine and human) in favor of Miaphysitism (that Christ has one united divine-human nature). This 'Monophysite' position led to the Coptic church's separation from the Byzantine imperial church — a split that still persists. The theological dispute was inseparable from Egyptian cultural identity and resentment of Byzantine imperial interference in Egyptian church affairs. Coptic Christians today number approximately 10–15 million in Egypt and form a diaspora worldwide. The Coptic language (the descendant of ancient Egyptian) is preserved in liturgical use, making it a living link to pharaonic civilization. Coptic monasticism — pioneered by Anthony the Great and Pachomius in the Egyptian desert in the 3rd–4th centuries — was the origin of all Christian monasticism.
